transcript.text: So, gotcha.
Yeah, that sounds good.
And it's so easy to travel around Europe.
So that sounds amazing.
So I took a look at your questions and thought it would be easier just to kind of chat through it or versus a bunch of technical tax jargon.
So do you want to share your screen and you can kind of run through it?
Yeah, yeah, for sure.
Just to give you a little bit of context, like this is, so I work for Growthx, an agency that's how it hired to develop their content strategy and I'm a managing editor at Growthx.
So I just want to get as much unique, insightful info to create actually worthwhile blog posts.
So that's the goal for today.
So yeah, I have a couple of questions in different categories.
So just feel free to just talk and I'll use the transcript.
Okay, cool.
And then if...
Any clarification on anything, just cut me off.
Of course, yeah.
But yeah, I did content at my previous firm.
And yeah, so I've done this before and I just kind of chat through it.
then someone very talented with writing, et cetera, like yourself, will spin up an amazing piece of content.
And usually before it gets published, if it's helpful, I take a quick look at the content just to, from a technical perspective, make sure it's correct.
Um, okay, cool.
Um, so I think this is a great topic to start with, uh, when these are all really good questions.

So the top, just starting at the top, um, R&D credit mistakes, I think, uh, the first thing to point out, uh, with the R&D credit is, you know, a tax credit is an offset of actual taxes and there are different types of taxes.
So, uh, with the R&D credit, there are two taxes you can offset.
You can offset income tax, uh, which is.
Tax based on profit.
And then you can offset payroll taxes, which is a tax based on wages paid.
So most startups, because they're not profitable, don't pay income tax.
So 90% plus of them use the R&D credit to offset payroll tax.
And in order to offset payroll tax, you need to file your tax returns on time.
If you file late, then you lose the R&D credit in terms of the payroll tax offset.
That is the biggest mistake that we see, is startups will file their tax returns late.
And then even though they qualify for the R&D credit, they can't use it.
Because the rule is if you file late, you can't use it to offset payroll tax.
So that's definitely the biggest mistake we see in the R&D credit area.
Moving on to the next question.
I think I would reformulate that question.
So there are kind of general CPAs and tax advisors, and then there are some CPAs and tax advisors.
That's specialized in startups.
So if it's a CPA, it says right now, do most startup CPAs get wrong?
I would just get rid of startup and just say, what do most CPAs get wrong when working with startups, right?
Things like if you're not familiar with the unique tax issues startups face, things that can get missed are the R&D tax credit, some specific rules around that.
There's also something called the R&D capitalization rules.
That can easily be missed if you're not specialized in working with startups.
83B elections can be missed if you're not familiar with them.
And then I would say foreign disclosures also get missed if you don't specialize in startups.
Those are all kind of unique tax issues that startups face.
would pharmaceuticals
But if you're just a general tax advisor that's not familiar with those issues, it can get missed.
So what are the consequences, like the most common consequences that you see when a startup works with a CPA that just doesn't really know how to work with startups?
Sometimes they will miss claiming the R&D credit.
Yeah.
Is a big one.
Or they'll miss a foreign disclosure.
And what I mean by that, a lot of startups will have an operational footprint outside of the U.S.
very early.
They'll have a foreign subsidiary in another country where they hire their engineering team.
Or the U.S.
company will be owned by a foreign parent.
And long story short, if you have any type of kind of non-U.S.
operational footprint, you have to.
File some special disclosures with the government, and that's not widely known unless you're working in that area, right?
So most kind of general companies in the U.S.
don't have foreign operations, so you're not going to be familiar with those unless you see it day in and day out like you do with startups.
Yeah.
So those are the consequences.
And then if you miss the foreign disclosures, the penalties start at $25,000 from the IRS.
So the penalties are pretty steep, even for startups.
Okay.
Which states have startup-friendly?
So 30-plus states have their own version of the earned credit, right?
So there's federal income tax, which we're talking about, and there's state tax.
So a lot of states also have their own version of earned credit.
And in a nutshell, that's just the state's way of incentivizing to...
the U.S.
Conductor R&D work within the state's boundaries, so that meaning you hire people in their state and that's good for the local autonomy, etc.
So that's the major state tax incentive for startups is the state version of the R&D credit.
And most states have some version of the credit.
Also, some states offer partial exemptions of sales tax for R&D equipment that's purchased.
So that's also another state incentive.
So those are the two major ones that jump out at me.
Okay.
Just moving on.
At what revenue funding stage do tax strategies typically change?
I would say it's a good question.
I would say once you raise your first or just prior to raising your first institute.
Right.
Pre-seed, seed funding.
You need a bit more strategy.
And typically what that looks like is someone will start up a company.
They'll just spin up an LLC for cheap on some platform.
And then, you know, they're doing their thing and then they decide to go out and raise investor capital.
And at that stage, before you will get the investor dollars, they'll typically mandate that you convert from an LLC to a Delaware seed corporation.
So I would say, kind of in general, the trigger for, you know, having more involved tax planning and strategies would be just prior to the first institutional round, whether that's pre-seed or seed.
So pretty early on.
At that point, they will, like, what do you, this is clearly not a finance, like I'm not, don't have any financial background and in companies, but what do like startups that are pre-seed?
Like, pre-seed or, like, pre-funding, what do they usually do when it comes to tax?
Like, do they work with a CPA?
Do they try to, like, do it themselves?
Like, what do you typically see?
Um, both.
So some, right, will try to do it on their own, just because budget is pretty tight in the early days.
And some will have an existing relationship with their local CPA or tax advisor, right, the person handling their personal taxes for use.
They'll just go to them to handle the business tax as well.

And then once they go out and start raising money from investors, typically they'll upgrade, right, based on the recommendation of their investors or attorneys to a tax advisor that's a bit more familiar with startup tax issues.
Okay.
Um, how, like, running your, you know, doing your own taxes for your own startups, for your own startup when you're, when you're pre-funding, um, would you say that
Like very time consuming or stressful for the founder, or is it something that kind of, they only think about when it's time to file their taxes?
Generally, they only think about it when it's time to file the taxes or they get a notice, some type of tax notice in the mail from the government.
I would say that's something they worry about, but it's something that causes stress, right?
They know they need to do something, and in general, it's known that if you file late, there are adverse consequences in terms of penalties.
So it can be time consuming, and it can be stressful for founders, especially if they're a first time founder, not familiar with kind of all the tax issues that they need to deal with.
There's so many of them in the U.S.
Payroll tax, Delaware franchise tax, income tax.
It's quite a bit sales tax, but I'd say it's a stressful.
Issues for founders to deal with, especially first-time founders.
Yep.
And then the last question, the first part, is lesser-known tax deductions for startups.
That thing jumps out at me there.
Okay.
The thing to be aware of there is just most startups operate at a loss.
So there's no real income tax for them because income tax is based on profit.
It's more of a compliance matter.
You just need to get the filings in on time every year.
So it's not, yeah, missing tax deductions generally is not an issue because there's no tax anyways.
But obviously the R&D credit, right, that's not a deduction, it's a credit.
That's one that could get missed if you're not working with the right advisor.
Yeah.
And that's money back in their pocket, so it's important.
Content gap analysis.
Questions, they ask frequently.
They ask questions.
Oh, gosh.
So many.

Entity structure, how should I set up my company, LLC, corporation, R&D tax credit is generally known within the startup community, so they'll have questions about that, they'll have questions about something called an 83B election, that's just a tax issue that is friendly to founders in terms of their shares, vets.
Yeah, multi-state issues, right?
Even early on, they tend to hire across state lines, so there's some tax issues there.
So those are some of the general questions that we get from founders.
Now, go ahead, please.

So, when it comes to advising them on the different types of entities that they could be registered as, can you just give me, like, a high-level overview of what you typically recommend, like, an LLC or an S-Corp or?
Sure.
So almost always, if there are investors involved, it's going to be a C corporation, typically in the state of Delaware.
That's just the structure that investors prefer for various reasons.
But if it's, sometimes you'll come across a founder, right, that's, you know, had an exit, and they don't necessarily need outside capital, so they'll fund the startup on their own.
In that instance, it could make sense to start off as an LLC, because the early losses would be deductible at the personal level.
The founder is personally funding it, and then later down the road, if they decide to take on institutional capital, potentially they'll flip to a C corporation, is what it typically looks like.
Okay, that makes sense.
When it comes to sort of multi-state operations, what are the most common...
And maybe not issues, but just, you know, things that businesses like startups have to deal with if they're going to be doing businesses like across the lines.
Oh, it's a pain.
So just registering with the state, right?
Once you start conducting business in the state, you have to go through this whole registration process and obtain state ID numbers.
That's a bit cumbersome.
Each state does it a bit differently.
So that's painful.
And then most states, regardless of profitability, even if you're in a loss, they'll impose some type of minimum tax.
So in California, it doesn't matter how early on you are or if you're generating revenue or profit, they'll charge $800 per year.
Massachusetts is $456 per year.
So in addition to registering with the state and filing in the state, which comes with the cost, you have to pay someone to file for you.
There's some type of minimum tax.
That the state's imposed as well.
And then just trying to navigate the rules per state, which differ, right?
The deadlines can be different.
The taxes are different.
The registrations are different.
It's just a compliance headache for founders is the main thing.
Just trying to be compliant in all the different states is a bit of a headache.
Okay.
That makes sense.
Misconceptions.
I think the main misconception is, you know, the fact that they just need to file.
So a lot of times when we're chatting with prospects, they'll say, oh, hey, we just formed a company, but we don't have any revenue.
So we don't need to file, right?
And the answer is no.
In general, once you establish a legal entity, you need to start filing an annual tax return.
So that's a misconception we commonly see is like they've formed the entity, but they don't have revenue.
So there's this assumption that since there's...
Money coming in, we don't need to file, and that's not, generally, that's not the case.
What advice do we give the consistently surprised as founders?
I don't know if I have an answer for that.
I guess your previous answer would count as that.
So basically, they're kind of shocked that they have to file with the IRS, even though they're pre-revenue.
I think that would also come under this.
Yeah, it's kind of the same question.
Yeah, I don't know if they're shocked.
But yeah, surprised, I would say sure.
yeah.

Today, I guess, if you've ever maybe worked with a startup that had CPA before, and then you come in and you try to get them to do things a different way, is there something that maybe you have seen that, you know, like,
Startups would do under guidance of a different CPA, that you're kind of like, okay, we should be doing this differently.
I think the misses that we see when we pick up clients from other firms that are not focused on startups are kind of the issues I flagged earlier.
The R&D credit and the foreign disclosures are the issues that are typically missed when we're picking up a client, you know, coming from a local firm that doesn't specialize in startups.
That's typically what we see.
Okay.
Yeah.
makes sense.
Yeah.
Recent tax law changes.
That's a really good one.
So a few years ago, there was a big law change.
So in the past, I'm sorry, I'm going to get a little technical.
could write off your R&D expenses.
That's different than the credit.
There's credits and expenses.
Those are two different things, right?
But you could.
For decades, could just write off your R&D expenses, which makes sense.
A few years ago, they changed it.
So instead of expensing it, you would have to do something called capitalize it, which just means you can't expense it in the current year.
You expense it over time.
So that was a massive change a few years ago that caused a lot of pain, right?
That caused taxes to be owed by startups, even though they're losing money in real life because of this tax law that requires you to capitalize.
They would have to pay tax.
So that was a law that changed a few years ago.
It caused a lot of pain.
There's a new tax bill in the U.S.
Congress floating around that will change that law back to what it used to be.
So instead of capitalizing it, you can now expense it.
And they may even make it retroactive, meaning you can go back and amend prior to your returns.
So that is a potential tax law change that would be massively impactful to startups.
Is what they're doing with.
The R&D deduction, right, expensing versus capitalizing.
So that's the major one for sure.
Are there any upcoming deadline changes?
Not that I'm aware of.
IRS guidance or audit trends.
Oh, that's a good question.
So the IRS, they're not known for being kind of at the forefront of technology, but I've been reading a bit that they're actually using AI to inform how they audit.
So I would, you could do a little research on that and write on that.
I think that's really interesting to write about.
Yeah, they are using AI to kind of inform who and why they audit companies.
Yeah, a quick side question.
If a startup gets, like, have you ever worked with a startup that was getting audited by the IRS?
Yes.
Like, I understand, like, you probably might not be able to go into details, but, like, just how this is.
Startup, like, approach that?
Like, what's the best way to just, like, tackle that scenario and, like, come out of it with a good outcome?
So definitely you want to engage the professional to represent you in the audit.
And typically, whoever prepared the return typically will represent you with the IRS.
So that's the most important thing, just to have professional representation.
And then the other thing is having good records, specifically accounting records.
So in an audit of a business, one of the things, one of the techniques is just to dive into the books and records of a company.
And if the books and records are messy, it's easy for them to come in and make changes and assess tax.
But if you have really clean books and records, everything ties out and is in order.
That makes it more challenging for them to, you know, make an adjustment and assess additional taxes.
Okay.
How are AI developments affecting R&D credit qualification?
I don't think there's anything noteworthy there.
Yeah, mean, if you're...
Yeah, I don't think there's anything noteworthy there.
Moving on to town, what does town provide to startups that makes them sign up immediately?

I think from what we've seen in the market, obviously, startups, when they hear about town's vision for using an AI-enabled platform to take a lot of the grunt work and heavy lifting out of tax preparation so that their tax advisor, the human in the loop, can spend more time with them on calls like this, I think that really resonates with them.
And obviously, they're tech savvy, so they just want to work with a provider that aligns with that.
But I would say that's what...
So that's a big...
That's the...
The biggest issue most businesses in general have with their tax professional, they're not proactive and they don't communicate as quickly as they would like, right?
You send an email to your tax advisor, you don't hear back for a few days or longer.
But with town, because we're leaning on AI, that's going to free up the time of our tax pros so that they can be proactive and more responsive to clients.
Yeah, a quick question.
So when you're sort of working with startups, what kind of work do you delegate to AI and then what's the work that you actually handle?
Yeah, great question.
So, you know, the platforms, it exists, but they haven't rolled it out yet.
The way it will work eventually, as of today, right, the technology is changing, so maybe this changes by year end.
But in general, kind of the way it should work is, you know, the client will upload their data to our platform.
So they'll connect their accounting system, like QuickBooks, they'll connect their payroll system to our platform, they'll connect other systems of records that they have to the town platform.
The town platform ingests that data, and then we'll, based on that, follow up with some questions, right?
A dynamic questionnaire, essentially, based on the data that's submitted.
You fill in the blanks, right?

Once we have that initial data and some questions answered by the client, they will then take that information that's stored in a data lake or data warehouse, and they will actually prepare a first draft of the tax return.

Then at that point, the draft gets sent to our tax managers, who will review it, follow up with any remaining questions with the client, and take the return across the finish line in terms of filing it with the government.
So that's what the problem
As we envision it will look like in a nutshell.
Okay, that's very helpful.
Thank you.
And yeah, the last couple of questions, basically, I think you kind of answered the second one, which when should startups kind of start working with town?
I assume probably like maybe even before they raise their first funding round or?
Yeah, I would say the sooner the better, because even early on, there are things you can do wrong that could be really painful from a tax perspective.
So the sooner the better.
Like the things that could go wrong, basically like the foreign disclosures that you mentioned.
Yeah.
Yeah, all the stuff I mentioned, missed R&D credit, foreign disclosures, missed setting up the wrong entity, right?
And then missing an 83B election.
That's a very common one.
Okay.
And then lastly, was just, I mean, I know a lot of.

Startups are very scrappy, but if a startup does have like an internal team that does their like maybe bookkeeping or just other financial activities, I was just wondering kind of how you plug in with them or, you know, how does that like collaboration happen?
Yeah, that's an important collaboration.
So typically the way we structure our engagements with town is once a client signs up, they get assigned a U.S.-based tax manager.
And, you know, the U.S.-based tax manager will work directly with the client's accountant to pull the information we need.
So typically the accountant has most of the data that we need to prepare the tax return.
So we'll work primarily with the accountant to pull all the information we need.
And then we'll have questions on the accounting.
So we typically work with their accountant really well.
And then, you know, sometimes we'll follow up directly with the

Client for information that they have that the accountant would, so to the extent possible, work directly with their accountant, so the client isn't burning time on tax preparation, but then for the last bits of information we need, we'll work directly with the client to kind of stop the eyes and prostitutes, that's typically what it looks like.
Okay, perfect.
Yeah, I think those are all the questions that I had.
Do you have anything else that you think is important to mention?
No, I think this is really good.
Once you start working on it, if you have follow-up questions, just feel free to ping me.
I'm happy to kind of chat or add my two cents into any document.
Great, perfect.

Now, mean, wherever we end up quoting you directly, I'll definitely share just to get your sign off on it, but yeah, think Jean-Denis, he added you to our Slack channel, so if it's okay, I can just send you a message there.
Yep, that's cool.
All right.
Well, thank you so much for your time.
I really, really appreciate it.
Yeah.
Have a great rest of your day.
Sounds good.
Thank you, Aida.
Take care.
Thank you.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Gather expert insights on startup tax strategies for content creation](https://fathom.video/share/o5JPt4JvY7asp_GAyn1MqWmqBmB5JH4k?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [R\&D credit misuse (late filing) is the biggest startup tax mistake, costing payroll tax offset opportunities](https://fathom.video/share/o5JPt4JvY7asp_GAyn1MqWmqBmB5JH4k?tab=summary&timestamp=162.0)
  - [Non-startup-specialized CPAs often miss crucial elements: R\&D credits, foreign disclosures, 83B elections](https://fathom.video/share/o5JPt4JvY7asp_GAyn1MqWmqBmB5JH4k?tab=summary&timestamp=200.0)
  - [Tax strategies typically evolve at pre-seed/seed stage, shifting from DIY/local CPA to specialized advisors](https://fathom.video/share/o5JPt4JvY7asp_GAyn1MqWmqBmB5JH4k?tab=summary&timestamp=415.0)
  - [Town.com leverages AI for efficient tax prep, freeing advisors for proactive, responsive client communication](https://fathom.video/share/o5JPt4JvY7asp_GAyn1MqWmqBmB5JH4k?tab=summary&timestamp=1249.0)

## Topics

### R\&D Credit Pitfalls and Opportunities

  - [Two tax offset types: income (profit-based) and payroll (wage-based)](https://fathom.video/share/o5JPt4JvY7asp_GAyn1MqWmqBmB5JH4k?tab=summary&timestamp=113.0)
  - [90%+ startups use R\&D credit for payroll tax offset due to lack of profitability](https://fathom.video/share/o5JPt4JvY7asp_GAyn1MqWmqBmB5JH4k?tab=summary&timestamp=131.0)
  - [Critical mistake: late tax filing nullifies R\&D credit's payroll tax offset benefit](https://fathom.video/share/o5JPt4JvY7asp_GAyn1MqWmqBmB5JH4k?tab=summary&timestamp=143.0)
  - [Potential $25k+ penalties for missed foreign disclosures (e.g., foreign subsidiaries/parent companies)](https://fathom.video/share/o5JPt4JvY7asp_GAyn1MqWmqBmB5JH4k?tab=summary&timestamp=323.0)

### State-Level Startup Incentives

  - [30+ states offer R\&D credit versions to incentivize local R\&D work](https://fathom.video/share/o5JPt4JvY7asp_GAyn1MqWmqBmB5JH4k?tab=summary&timestamp=341.0)
  - [Some states provide partial sales tax exemptions on R\&D equipment purchases](https://fathom.video/share/o5JPt4JvY7asp_GAyn1MqWmqBmB5JH4k?tab=summary&timestamp=383.0)

### Startup Tax Evolution

  - [Pre-seed/seed funding triggers more complex tax planning](https://fathom.video/share/o5JPt4JvY7asp_GAyn1MqWmqBmB5JH4k?tab=summary&timestamp=415.0)
  - [Common progression: LLC formation → Delaware C-Corp conversion (investor mandate)](https://fathom.video/share/o5JPt4JvY7asp_GAyn1MqWmqBmB5JH4k?tab=summary&timestamp=426.0)
  - [Early-stage options: DIY (budget constraints) or existing personal CPA relationship](https://fathom.video/share/o5JPt4JvY7asp_GAyn1MqWmqBmB5JH4k?tab=summary&timestamp=491.0)
  - [Post-funding: Often upgrade to startup-specialized tax advisors (investor/attorney recommendation)](https://fathom.video/share/o5JPt4JvY7asp_GAyn1MqWmqBmB5JH4k?tab=summary&timestamp=512.0)

### Multi-State Operations Challenges

  - [Registration complexities: Each state has unique processes and ID number requirements](https://fathom.video/share/o5JPt4JvY7asp_GAyn1MqWmqBmB5JH4k?tab=summary&timestamp=794.0)
  - [Minimum taxes: Many states impose fees regardless of profitability (e.g., CA: $800/year, MA: $456/year)](https://fathom.video/share/o5JPt4JvY7asp_GAyn1MqWmqBmB5JH4k?tab=summary&timestamp=806.0)
  - [Compliance headache: Navigating varied state-specific rules, deadlines, and filing requirements](https://fathom.video/share/o5JPt4JvY7asp_GAyn1MqWmqBmB5JH4k?tab=summary&timestamp=842.0)

### Common Misconceptions and Surprises

  - [Belief that no revenue means no filing requirement (false; entity formation typically necessitates annual filing)](https://fathom.video/share/o5JPt4JvY7asp_GAyn1MqWmqBmB5JH4k?tab=summary&timestamp=550.0)
  - [Shock at needing to file with IRS pre-revenue](https://fathom.video/share/o5JPt4JvY7asp_GAyn1MqWmqBmB5JH4k?tab=summary&timestamp=559.0)

### Recent and Potential Tax Law Changes

  - [R\&D expense treatment shift: From immediate write-off to capitalization (caused unexpected startup taxes)](https://fathom.video/share/o5JPt4JvY7asp_GAyn1MqWmqBmB5JH4k?tab=summary&timestamp=1010.0)
  - [Potential reversal in new tax bill: Back to immediate expensing, possibly retroactive](https://fathom.video/share/o5JPt4JvY7asp_GAyn1MqWmqBmB5JH4k?tab=summary&timestamp=1059.0)
  - [IRS adopting AI for audit targeting and execution](https://fathom.video/share/o5JPt4JvY7asp_GAyn1MqWmqBmB5JH4k?tab=summary&timestamp=1094.0)

### Town.com's Unique Value Proposition

  - [AI-enabled platform reduces grunt work in tax preparation](https://fathom.video/share/o5JPt4JvY7asp_GAyn1MqWmqBmB5JH4k?tab=summary&timestamp=1313.0)
  - [Increased advisor availability for strategic discussions and responsive communication](https://fathom.video/share/o5JPt4JvY7asp_GAyn1MqWmqBmB5JH4k?tab=summary&timestamp=1260.0)
  - [Data ingestion from various systems (e.g., QuickBooks, payroll) for streamlined preparation](https://fathom.video/share/o5JPt4JvY7asp_GAyn1MqWmqBmB5JH4k?tab=summary&timestamp=1313.0)
  - [AI-drafted returns with human review and finalization](https://fathom.video/share/o5JPt4JvY7asp_GAyn1MqWmqBmB5JH4k?tab=summary&timestamp=1346.0)

## Next Steps

  - [Research and elaborate on IRS's use of AI in auditing processes](https://fathom.video/share/o5JPt4JvY7asp_GAyn1MqWmqBmB5JH4k?tab=summary&timestamp=1094.0)
  - [Follow up with Anjum for any clarifications during content creation](https://fathom.video/share/o5JPt4JvY7asp_GAyn1MqWmqBmB5JH4k?tab=summary&timestamp=1533.0)
  - [Share direct quotes with Anjum via Slack for approval before publication](https://fathom.video/share/o5JPt4JvY7asp_GAyn1MqWmqBmB5JH4k?tab=summary&timestamp=1543.0)
  - [Consider creating content on optimal timing for startups to engage specialized tax services](https://fathom.video/share/o5JPt4JvY7asp_GAyn1MqWmqBmB5JH4k?tab=summary&timestamp=1402.0)

action_items.description: Review draft blog post content from Aida for technical accuracy pre-publication
Share direct quotes from interview with Anjum for approval before publication
